data.title: Did Walmart Take A Shot Across The Bow?
data.description:
Walmart (WMT) is considered a bellwether for consumer spending, which is why its disappointing outlook for sales in 2025 weighed heavily on the broad market yesterday. The shares were down more than 6%. Despite impressive fourth-quarter results that exceeded estimates on the top and bottom lines, management’s forecast for sales growth of 3-4% for the current year fell short of the 4% expected. In my view, this was hardly bad news, as the company provided nearly the same guidance a year ago. As for the drop in the stock price, Walmart trades at an absurdly expensive 38 times this year’s consensus earnings estimate of $2.74 per share. This is an example of where the stock market is grossly overvalued, so the guidance looks like more of an excuse to take profits than anything else, but there was a word of caution from management worth noting.

CFO John Rainey indicated that the guidance of 3-4% in revenue growth does not include the adverse impact of tariffs if implemented as proposed. Walmart imports food from Mexico and general merchandise from China to obtain low prices for its customers. Still, the company typically guides very conservatively to start a new year, and 3-4% is more consistent with trend growth of 2% for the economy, which is what we should expect during a mid-cycle slowdown. Granted, consumer confidence fell to start the year, and retail sales were weaker than expected, but I don’t view this as the shot across the bow some more pessimistic are making it out to be.

Yesterday, we also learned that the Conference Board’s Leading Economic Index (LEI) fell by 0.3% in January to a reading of 101.5, but the 0.9% decline over the past six months is less than the 1.7% decline over the previous six months in what is a positive rate of change. January’s decline was due to the deterioration in consumer sentiment and fewer manufacturing hours worked. Yet new orders in the manufacturing sector have improved more recently, and six of the 10 components of the LEI were positive in January. Additionally, the six-month and 12-month growth rates are trending up, leading to a GDP forecast of 2.3% in 2025.

The threat of new trade policies that could be inflationary and hamper our rate of economic growth is real, but until substantiated they only serve to feed uncertainty. That uncertainty increases volatility, but it does not disrupt the uptrend in the major market indexes. It is not an ideal scenario, but investors better get used to it for the next four years. As the rate of economic growth slows this year, as intended by the Federal Reserve to reduce the rate of inflation, the punditry will repeatedly make calls for a downturn in the economy and an end to the bull market. It will be important to distinguish the difference between a deceleration in rates of growth and when that deceleration is the precursor to a downturn. I don’t see a downturn on the horizon yet.

data.title: Ryerson Holding Corporation 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Ryerson Holding Corporation in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: CarGurus: Rocky Recovery Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CarGurus, Inc. (CARG) is a global online automotive platform that helps connect buyers and sellers. It is the industry-leading listing marketplace with both digital retail solutions and the CarOffer online wholesale platform.

The platform has been growing ever since it was founded in 2006 because it brings greater trust and transparency to the automotive market. In 2023, the platform brought in over 30 million average monthly visitors in the U.S. and over 24,000 paying dealers.

After going through crazy ups and downs during the pandemic/post-pandemic era, used car values have finally been stabilizing during 2024, and I expect that to continue going forward. While CarGurus’ financial performance sharply deteriorated during 2023 and early 2024, it has reversed its course in 2024 as the used and new car market smooths out.

I expect CarGurus to continue their recovery because:

  • CarGurus has the best Return On Investment for independent dealers, which will attract more dealers and bring larger inventory to the platform.
  • CarGurus gross profit margin and EBITDA margin are increasing, which indicate the stabilization of their operations and finances.
  • CarGurus marketplace growth is accelerating, and I expect the growth to continue in the foreseeable future.

Best Return On Investment for Dealers

Since the acquisition of CarOffer in 2021, CarGurus has been increasing efficiencies for dealers to source vehicles from both consumers and dealers via the online CarOffer Matrix. The dealers leverage CarGurus’ large consumer audience, digital retail offerings, and consumer trade-in services to generate higher quality leads, which results in higher Return On Investment for dealers.

CarGurus is ranked #1 in Return On Investment among listings sites by Marketprobe, and their Quarterly Average Revenue Per Subscribing Dealers (QARSD) has been steadily increasing. In 4Q 2024, the QARSD was $6.1 M, which represented 11.6% YOY growth.

CarGurus recently launched Next Best Deal Rating that features artificial intelligence-powered insights to analyze a dealer’s inventory and make suggestions to achieve a higher deal rating on the CarGurus platform.

The dealer bases have been growing both in the U.S. and internationally, and I expect the trend to continue in the future.

Recovering Profit Margins

The used vehicle market went through turmoil in the past several years. Right after the pandemic, used vehicle sales fell off and used car value dropped along with the rest of the economy. Thereafter, the combination of numerous factors (e.g., supply chain disruption, chip shortage, increased new car prices, etc.) drove up the used car prices insanely high for a couple of years.

During 2022 and 2023, the supply chain started to normalize, and used vehicle sales started to decline. The values started to stabilize during 2024, and I expect that to be the case going forward.

After going through a rough patch in 2022 and 2023, CarGurus has been managing to recover, and it is showing in their profitability metrics. Gross profit improved from $168 M in 4Q 2023 to $196 M in 4Q 2024, and gross profit margin improved from 75% in 4Q 2023 to 87% in 4Q 2024. EBITDA also improved from $61.2 M in 4Q 2023 to $76.4 M in 4Q 2024, and the EBITDA margin increased from 27% in 4Q 2023 to 33% in 4Q 2024.

During the previous earnings call, management mentioned that the gross margin expansion was driven by the favorable revenue mix shift toward the high margin marketplace business. I expect the profitability to continue to improve as this marketplace grows in the future.

Accelerating Growth of Marketplace

CarGurus manages to deliver value to dealer partners and consumers, enabling them to gain market share in the auto marketplace industry. CarGurus’ marketplace visitor metrics demonstrate their market-leading consumer experience, which is one of the key reasons for their momentum in marketplace revenue acceleration.

CarGurus has 64% more average monthly visits than their closest competitor, and a visitor to CarGurus’ site spends 37% more minutes on average than their closest competitors.

Furthermore, CarGurus has 20% more inventory than their next largest competitor.

All these factors have been contributing to the accelerating CarGurus’ marketplace revenue. The marketplace revenue grew from $182 M in 4Q 2023 to $210 M in 4Q 2024, which represents 15.3% YOY growth. Also, the revenue grew each quarter during the span ($182M, $187M, $195M, $204 M, and $210 M in 4Q 2023, 1Q 2024, 2Q 2024, 3Q 2024, and 4Q 2024 respectively).

According to the previous earnings call, management attributed the growth to the global expansion of the new dealer base, migration of higher subscription tiers, and adoption of value-added products.

Financial Highlights from 4Q 2024 Earnings

The last earnings call was a bag of mixed messages. The total 4Q 2024 revenue was $228.5 M. This was lower than the market estimate of $232.8 M. However, EPS of $0.55 was higher than the analyst estimates of $0.52, and EBITDA came in at $76.4 M. This was higher than the market estimate of $75.75 M. The number of paying dealers increased by 1,075 to 32,010.

Overall, revenue was slightly lower than expected, but the profitability seemed to continue to improve. Operating margin improved from -10% last year in the same quarter to 23.3%, and free cash flow margin also increased from 17.9% in the previous quarter to 27.1%.

Management remarked on the significant margin expansion across geographies, which is very positive news for their outlook.

Risk

As inflation stays high and the Federal Reserve has maintained a high-interest rate to fight inflation, consumer credit started showing signs of deterioration among subprime burrowers. After dipping to about 15% in 2022, the credit card delinquency rate among subprime borrowers rose above the pre-pandemic level. The revolving balance percentage also started rising since 2022.

These are typically the early signs of an economic slowdown, and this would negatively impact CarGurus’ recovery. Therefore, investors should monitor the macroeconomic conditions.

Even though CarGurus has been successfully recovering from a dismal 2022 and 2023, it is still not consistently profitable yet (i.e., net income near breakeven for trailing twelve month). As mentioned above, the profitability metric is improving, and, with the revenue growth and favorable revenue mix, I expect them to achieve net income profitability soon. However, the road to the recovery is certainly not guaranteed, and the stock price might swing wildly until then. Investors should be aware of the short-term volatility of the stock.

Conclusion

The pandemic brought a roller coaster to many parts of our lives and economy, and the used vehicle market is certainly one example. I remember receiving a ridiculous number of offer letters and phone calls from car dealerships that wanted to buy my car. Then the market condition changed in 2022, and the used car prices started to go down.

CarGurus experienced that wild ride at the front-line, and I believe they managed it pretty well.

CarGurus' revenue has been growing nicely in the past several quarters, and their profitability metrics are improving. With continued strong performance from their marketplace and favorable revenue mix, I expect them to continue their recovery. The deteriorating consumer credit and a potential slowdown in the economy could challenge the company, but I expect them to be able to handle it.

I believe CarGurus will be a great recovery story.

data.title: Innodata: Short-Term Momentum Play
data.description:
The Innodata Investment Thesis

On a pure growth basis, Innodata Inc. (NASDAQ:INOD) is a very interesting play right now. Because growth seems to be exploding and therefore momentum is on Innodata's side. In my opinion, momentum has been the most successful strategy in recent years, and I think it will continue to be in 2025. At the same time, however, I also believe that there is a lot of uncertainty in the long term and that it is almost impossible to predict whether Innodata's business model will continue to be as promising in the future.

Therefore, a small position in Innodata is interesting for me as a short-term momentum play. However, I would quickly close the position on any weakness.

Innodata's Q4/24 and FY24 Results

Innodata has been active in the field of machine learning and artificial intelligence for some time, as the image above shows. And here they are very much focused on labeling, fine-tuning, and testing of models. This includes the testing and development of prompts, but also the fine-tuning of data sets and also red teaming, which is a form of cybersecurity testing.

They also have Synodex, a kind of medical data platform, and Agility PR Solutions, which deals with media intelligence.

And if we look at these segments over FY24, we see that Synodex grew only slightly from $7,511 to $7,864, while the other two segments grew significantly more. The core and most prominent segment is, of course, DDS, which includes AI activities. This segment grew by over 120% from $61,576 to $141,098.

Following a strong Q4 with revenue growth of 127%, FY24 revenue grew 96% from $86,775 to $170,461.

I find the development of net income even more pleasing and more important for the momentum. Despite the strong growth, net income has developed positively. And that is extraordinary. Not many companies manage to post a positive net income with this kind of growth. Often this only happens after several years of strong growth.

This positive development is due to the fact that selling and administrative expenses did not increase at the same rate as sales, which had a positive impact on margins. While sales have almost doubled, administrative costs have only risen by around 38%. That shows efficiency.

I also find it positive that SBCs have actually come down compared to FY2013, which is unusual for a growth company. Often the opposite is the case, and SBC costs start out incredibly high and only come down as growth slows. However, Innodata's decline is minimal, from $4,027 to $3,998, so it's probably more like SBC is relatively flat year over year.

Another positive development is that institutional ownership seems to have increased, as you can see from the following tweet on the X platform.

The market opportunities and growth rates presented in the investor presentation also look very good. And I also see the fact that many companies have forecast very high CapEx expenditures for AI as a positive signal for Innodata. But how much of this growth is realistically achievable, and what role Innodata will play in the rapidly changing AI market, is not really foreseeable at this time.

It may well be that Innodata grows strongly for another 1–2 years and then stops growing abruptly because the landscape has changed completely. However, I think the 40% growth guidance for FY25 is a little on the low side because the guidance for 2024 has always been on the low side and has been beaten and raised several times. For example, in Q3 they were expecting 88% to 92% growth in Q4, and it ended up being 96%.

I am always a big believer in Recurring Revenue or ARR, and in Innodata's case, I would like to have more information. In my opinion, Innodata's investment presentations could be a bit more comprehensive. I think more detail on the segments and a little more information would build trust.

I see Innodata as more of a services company as of Q3 they generated 89% of their revenue from services and only 11% from SaaS in the Agility Media segment. And I think the revenue from consulting is not as sticky as the revenue from long-term contracts for mission-critical programs. And for long-term investments, I always prefer companies that are essential to their customers, and I do not see that in Innodata yet.

Balance Sheet

Since Innodata has had negative net debt for several years, meaning that its cash position is greater than its financial obligations, and since the cash position increased to $46.9 million in the last quarter, I consider the balance sheet to be secure. But $46 million is also not much when it comes to thinking about spending it on M&A or making big investments. Therefore, I consider the business to be secured with the cash position, but it does not provide much room for growth outside of organic growth.

Valuation

If we compare Innodata with some of the other companies that have also been very momentum-driven over the last few years or months and have benefited from that, we see that Innodata does not really seem overvalued. The other high-flyers are in a similar PS multiple range. Palantir's (PLTR) multiple is way ahead of the pack, but of course, that is also due to the fact that Palantir is much better known and bigger in social media. If Innodata were to get more social media attention, I could see the multiple expanding again in the short term. Social media hype can have a significant impact on multiples these days.

Risks

Short reports do not always have to be true, and many companies have done great despite short reports, such as Tesla (TSLA), but one should always be careful that they do not derail one's investment case. Two of Innodata's well-known short reports are those from Wolfpack Research and J Capital Research. J Capital's latest report alleges that revenues may have been overstated and that the auditor is an Indian company.

That is to say: I would recommend anyone interested in Innodata to read these reports and then make up their own mind about the situation.

Conclusion

Right now, I see Innodata as a consulting company that is growing fast and has momentum on its side. But if there really is a competitive advantage that will protect the revenues in the future, I think one cannot really say. Yes, Innodata is growing extremely fast, which indicates that they are doing something right at the moment, but will they be able to protect their expertise from the mega-caps in the future, without the mega-caps doing it just as well or better themselves?

Because if your customers are some of the biggest companies in the world, they usually have some of the best engineers in the world, and they can do reverse engineering to figure out why something works. I mean, that is the business model in China, and it seems to be working well. Therefore, I think Innodata is interesting as a short-term momentum play as there seems to be room to run, but long-term there seem to be too many points that could break the investment case for me.

data.title: Meta Platforms: The Recent Breakout From $600 To $700 Is Just The Beginning
data.description:
It's been a great year for Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) shareholders, as shares extended their previous rally by an additional 49.18%. The stock price has come a long way, and investors who went against the grain in November of 2022 when META's stock price dipped below $100 have been rewarded handsomely as shares of META have climbed over 675% as they have increased from around $90 to over $700. I am still very bullish on META, and while some investors may be taking profits, I believe it's one of the more undervalued stocks in the Magnificent Seven. One of the big investing themes that, I think, will play out over the next several years is that big tech will just get bigger, and I am not looking to play that catch-up trade in small caps. META has more than 1/3rd of the global population using its products on a daily basis, and when businesses look to advertise, META's Platforms offer one of the best paths to their target audience. META has a lot of opportunities on the horizon that should drive revenue growth and EPS, including wearables, platforms, and generative AI. The share price could decline a bit from here, but META's focus on utilizing generative AI to enhance its product offerings should create a lot of value for shareholders going forward. I am still very bullish on META, and I think their best days are ahead of them.

Following up on my previous article about Meta Platforms

The last time I wrote an article about META was in the middle of August (can be read here). Since then, shares of META have appreciated by 33.82% compared to the S&P 500, which has climbed 13.29%. When the dividend is taken into account, shares of META have increased by 24.06% over this period. I had previously discussed how META was driving increased levels of revenue and profitability from its operations and the impacts of its advancements in AI from Q2 earnings. A lot has changed since then. As the Fed started cutting rates, a new administration took office, and META continued to increase its revenue and profitability. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still very bullish on META after shares have increased by almost 50% in the past year. I think there is still a significant opportunity on the horizon for META as they are on the forefront of the AI revolution, and the surface has only begun to be scratched.

Risks to investing in Meta Platforms

While I am bullish on META, and it's one of the largest companies in the market, there are still significant risk factors that investors should be aware of. META has been at the center of negative publicity in the past, which has impacted its share price. If they receive unfavorable media coverage around content handling, it could reduce confidence in META's brands, which could impact its business. META has a treasure trove of information on individuals and businesses, which makes it a target for cyberattacks. If META incurs a cyber breach, it could compromise user data, impact META's reputation, and lead to a loss of revenue. META is one of the largest advertising businesses, and its competitors are continuously trying to figure out ways to penetrate its moat. Amazon (AMZN) and Netflix (NFLX) have both tried to figure out ways to boost their advertising dollars as they have valuable space on their platforms and large amounts of traffic using their app and websites each day. If competitors are able to provide a better ROI than META on advertising dollars, then it would be difficult for META to retain users. There is also a risk from generative AI because META is allocating tens of billions toward R&D, and its efforts could get upstaged by competitors. Investors should do their own due diligence as there are many risk factors regarding allocating capital toward META.

Why I believe Meta Platforms is still undervalued after the recent breakout

META finished 2024 in a position of strength, and after going through their financials, there is a lot to be excited about. META finished the year with $77.82 billion in cash on hand between their marketable securities and cash equivalents. They also have an additional 6.07 billion in non-marketable equity securities, with only $28.83 billion in long-term debt. META operates a cash-rich business, and its balance sheet is one of the strongest in the market. META's financials put them in a position where they can ride out difficult macroeconomic events, and the fluctuations in interest rates don't necessarily impact them directly. META has built a company that is generating $62.36 billion of net income and has a return on equity (ROE) of 34.14%. META continues to generate excessive amounts of cash from its operations, which is allowing them to organically grow and drive value for shareholders.

META has been able to drive larger amounts of revenue from its platforms by increasing its ability to drive value from its ads. META's ad impressions increased by 11% YoY, with substantial growth from Asia-Pacific and Rest of World regions. While increasing its ad impressions, META also increased the price per ad by 10% in 2024. META's improvements to ad performance through enhanced targeting and measurement tools created a higher demand for its ads, and companies were willing to absorb the increases. META has also leveraged AI to improve its ad delivery, targeting, and measurement capabilities. This had a drastic impact on the financials in 2024 as META's revenue increased by $29.6 billion (21.94%) YoY to $164.5 billion. META was already a company that was generating more than $100 billion in revenue, and the demand for their services was so high that they witnessed an increase of more than 20% YoY. META was also able to drive higher margins from its business as it had a 37.91% profit margin, which increased from 28.98% YoY. META's profitability grew by 59.5% YoY as it generated $62.36 billion in net income. META's ability to increase revenue and profitability is a testament to its business's staying power and how its platforms provide value to its users.

Some investors and media outlets have questioned META's allocation toward CapEx, yet I am not concerned. I believe anyone who is worried about a $65 billion allocation toward CapEx in 2025 isn't looking at the bigger picture. On the Q4 conference call, Susan Li (META CFO) discussed the $65 billion outlay for CapEx that META was budgeting for in 2025. The YoY increase of roughly $25 billion is to support META's generative AI efforts and its core businesses. Over the past decade, META has directed $172.1 billion toward CapEx, and while there have been questions regarding the large spend, the results have been accreditive for shareholders. Spending $172.1 billion on CapEx has allowed META to generate $425.68 billion in cash from operations over the same period. META has produced $253.47 billion in free cash flow (FCF), which has allowed it to repurchase $189.99 billion worth of shares since 2015 and pay $5.07 billion in dividends in 2024. META has established a track record of growing its cash from operations and FCF as the investments into CapEx increase. I believe that the investments META is making will make its products more alluring to its customers and drive additional profitability from its operations.

When I look at how the market is valuing META compared to Magnificent Seven and companies such as Walmart Inc. (WMT) and Costco Wholesale (COST), I think there is still value that needs to be unlocked. META is trading at 27.55 times 2025 earnings and is expected to grow its earnings by 33.28% over the next 2 years, which places its 2027 P/E at 20.67. META is trading at the 2nd lowest valuation in the Magnificent Seven on both 2025 and 2027 earnings. The fact that WMT is being valued at 31.38 times 2027 earnings when they are expected to generate 24.5% of EPS growth over the next 2 years is an indication that META trading at 20.67 times 2027 earnings is an opportunity when META is projected to grow its EPS by 33.28% over the same period. When I look at the way META allocates capital and how they are able to grow profitability, there is no question in my mind that, over the long term, META's shares will continue to appreciate.

There are several opportunities that I am excited about that, I believe, will drive revenue higher for Meta Platforms

The massive amount of CapEx being allocated is allowing META to make significant advances across its family of apps. Through Meta AI, the user experience will improve through leveraging user data to focus on personalization. Meta AI will utilize specific details shared by users in one-on-one chats to personalize its responses and improve the algorithm. Meta AI will pull from all of its apps to improve content recommendations as it will have a more comprehensive understanding of the user's interests and preferences. If Meta AI can improve META's platforms and create longer engagement, it could charge more for advertising, which would likely increase the top and bottom line.

The opportunities in AI are extensive for META, as they are innovating in AI technologies to build new experiences across its products. It looks like META will focus on generative AI to make its platforms more immersive as they are creating tools to improve ad delivery, targeting, and measurement capabilities. META believes that its personal AI assistant will reach over 1 billion people on the commercial side, while internally, META believes it will be possible to build AI engineering agents that can code at the same level as mid-level engineers. It's been controversial, but moving beyond the traditional 2D screen has been debated for quite some time. I think that the inroads that META has created will allow it to be the frontrunner in a new computing frontier where users will experience virtual, mixed, and augmented reality through wearable devices. This is META's chance to own the hardware and be the tollbooth that app creators pay a fee to, unlike how META's products are currently situated with Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Apple (AAPL). In 2025, META expects to spend approximately 50% of Reality Labs operating expenses on wearables initiatives and 50% on metaverse initiatives. This could pay off in spades if widespread adoption occurs, as META will be a winner that takes most in the next generation of computing.

Conclusion

While META has experienced a multi-year bull run since November of 2022, I don't think the party is over. I believe that shares of META are undervalued as they are trading at less than 28 times 2025 earnings and 21 times 2027 earnings. The analyst community expects 33.28% EPS growth from the end of 2025 through 2027, and there could be more growth on the horizon as the wearable division expands. All of the CapEx that has been allocated to META's products has allowed them to generate larger levels of profitability and direct more capital toward share repurchases and dividends. META has a long history of increasing profitability, and when I look at their previous endeavors, the $65 billion being spent on CapEx in 2025 could have strong ramifications on their future earnings potential. I am still very bullish on META and think that shares will see $1,000 over the next 2 years.

data.title: Allspring Special Large Cap Value Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Equity markets and large-cap value stocks experienced significant volatility during the quarter. The first two months of the quarter saw the Russell 1000 Value Index move higher, as confidence around a “soft landing” increased following the Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts and optimism around a Trump election win. This optimism began to diminish in late November as investors began to discount a potentially slower and shallower rate cutting cycle and a December rally in the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y). The Russell 1000 Value Index returned -1.98% for the quarter. Within the index, the financials (+7%) and communication services (+2%) sectors were the best performing in the quarter. Meanwhile, the materials (-12%) and health care (-10%) sectors were the worst-performing sectors within the index.

The fund underperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as the early-quarter rally was led by the higher beta and more cyclical portions of the index that investors believed would benefit from the transition in both monetary and fiscal policy. However, the portfolio outperformed the index in the December pull back, as we expect our process to do, as well as for the full-year period.

*Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is noguarantee of future results, and donotreflect taxes a shareholder maypay on an investment in a fund. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance maybe lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.84%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.50%. The manager has contractually committed, through November 30, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.50% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Stock selection in communication services and materials contributed. Within communication services, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG,GOOGL) , the parent company of Google, provides the dominant search advertising platform, Android smartphone software, YouTube, and the Google Cloud platform. Some positive returns on Alphabet's significant investments into artificial intelligence have shown through with accelerating growth in search and cloud revenue, leading to the stock’s strong performance. We remain confident in management’s consistent investments in its innovation and technology advancements that provide multiple avenues of revenue growth, which, combined with a pristine balance sheet and ongoing cost optimization, result in an attractive reward/risk valuation. Stock selection in consumer staples and industrials detracted. In the industrials sector, Canadian Pacific Kansas City Ltd. (CP, CPKC) operates a transnational rail network. Through the acquisition of Kansas City Southern Railroad, CPKC’s network now offers the only single-line haul service connecting Canada to the U.S. and down through Mexico. We expect its lines to be among the lowest-cost and energy-efficient shipping options for bulk cargo in North America, and this advantage should allow the consolidation of market share to CPKC over time. CPKC underperformed the market as fears around Trump's trade policy and its potential impact on cross-border rail volumes garnered attention. We believe the sentiment swoon to be an overreaction.

Portfolio Positioning: Trailing 12-Month Attribution Analysis

The fund outpaced its benchmark over the 12-month period, driven primarily by stock selection. The Russell 1000 Value Index returned 14.37%, despite significant volatility throughout the year. Stock selection in real estate, consumer discretionary, and materials added value. A large contributor was General Motors Co. (GM), an American auto manufacturer. GM has a leading market-share position in large pickup trucks that carries pricing power and generates strong free cash flow to help facilitate balance sheet deployment to pursue organic growth opportunities. GM continued to exceed expectations as it focuses on profitability. GM also committed to prudent spending on capital. Instead of additional investments, GM believes a better use of its balance sheet is to return capital to shareholders.

Stock selection in energy and health care were the largest detractors. Within health care, Humana Inc. (HUM) is a large managed-care organization. Management recently reported an unexpected loss, lowered guidance for fiscal-year 2024, and guided to a weaker range for 2025 earnings-per-share growth due to higher-than-expected costs in the Medicare Advantage sector. Also, regulatory trends and political rhetoric in the industry have been a headwind; however, Humana has maintained a strong underlying cash flow profile, and we believe it should be able to price insurance plans for 2025 and start to recover this margin compression.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The fund made minor changes to positioning during the three-month period. The fund increased its weight in industrials and information technology and decreased it in real estate and materials as reward/risk ratios dictated. We expect continued market volatility in 2025 given the increased uncertainty around future fiscal and monetary policy. The new administration has brought some optimism around deregulation and an extension of previous corporate tax cuts; however, there is still much uncertainty around the impact of increased tariffs and rising geopolitical tensions. The path of future interest rate cuts could be slower and shallower than investor expectations as the Fed digests evolving labor and inflation data points. This macroeconomic uncertainty only strengthens our view that portfolios should be balanced with sound risk controls. Our portfolio construction process is designed to isolate stock selection as the primary source of projected tracking error and relative returns. We believe that companies will need to increasingly rely on their balance sheets to navigate the macro uncertainty. Pricing power will also be essential to protect margins and sustain cash flows due to rising tariff pressures, labor challenges, and supply chain modifications.

data.title: Renewable And Regulatory Uncertainty Suggests Continuing Trouble For CVR Energy
data.description:
Just a little over a year ago I suggested that CVR Energy (NYSE:CVI) might be worth buying for strong total returns due to improving refining crack spreads (at the time), and biodiesel fuel investments, along with improving fertilizer prices in early 2024. In addition, I had noted that famed investor Carl Icahn, who is part-owner of the company along with Icahn Enterprise (IEP) and CVR Partners (UAN), had a large ownership stake in CVI. This was what I wrote back in January 2024:

Icahn holds about 66% of the outstanding shares (units) of CVR Energy, even after selling about 4M units back in September. CVI is mostly a refiner and compared to its peers it has performed much better over the past 10 years.

Then in November 2024, Icahn boosted his stake in CVI up to 80% by cutting the IEP distribution in half.

At the time I had also noted that CVR Energy owned 37% of the units of CVR Partners and that rising fertilizer prices in the first half of 2023 had started to decline later in the year and were negatively impacting the UAN stock price, which in turn caused CVI stock to decline along with it.

CVR Energy also owns the general partner and 37% of the units of CVR Partners, so the performance of UAN stock and the nitrogen fertilizer business also directly impacts CVI. Fertilizer prices were recovering in 2023 up until about the end of October but have been sliding since then as shown on this price chart from YCharts.

However, in the year since I wrote that article with a Buy recommendation for CVI, the price and total return for UAN far outperformed CVI. In fact, UAN delivered a reasonable 1-year return (as of 2/20/25) of about 23% compared to a loss of -38% from CVI.

A big reason for that significant decline in CVI stock in 2024 was due to the failed $8B bid for Citgo followed by a suspension of the dividend. I downgraded my CVI rating to a Sell in October 2024 based on that news and other macro factors that spelled trouble for CVR Energy heading into 2025.

CVR Energy Q4 2024 Earnings Summary

This week CVR Energy reported Q4 2024 earnings on February 18 and the market responded positively. Even though revenues declined by -11% and earnings were negative, the report beat estimates on both the top and bottom line. Perhaps investors are now hoping that the bottom is in, and the current price offers a buying opportunity.

Here is a summary of the Q4 report from the press release:

CVR Energy, Inc. today announced fourth quarter 2024 net income attributable to CVR Energy stockholders of $28 million, or 28 cents per diluted share, compared to fourth quarter 2023 net income attributable to CVR Energy stockholders of $91 million, or 91 cents per diluted share. Adjusted loss for the fourth quarter of 2024 was 13 cents per diluted share compared to adjusted earnings of 65 cents per diluted share in the fourth quarter of 2023. Net income for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $40 million, compared to net income of $97 million in the fourth quarter of 2023. Fourth quarter 2024 EBITDA was $122 million, compared to fourth quarter 2023 EBITDA of $204 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $67 million, compared to adjusted EBITDA of $170 million in the fourth quarter of 2023.

In addition, the company enhanced liquidity by $408m in Q4 with a new term loan and the sale of a 50 percent interest in Midway Pipeline. The company generated free cash flow of $40 million in Q4. Total liquidity stood at $1.1 billion as of the end of 2024.

Admittedly, refining market conditions remained difficult in Q4 as explained by CEO Dave Lamp on the earnings call, however, the outlook for 2025 was cautiously optimistic:

Refining market conditions remained challenging in the fourth quarter, largely due to the market being oversupplied as a result of both average utilization levels in the United States as well as addition of new refining capacity globally. As we look to 2025, however, we are cautiously optimistic that refining market conditions will improve relative to 2024 for a number of reasons.

The CVI stock price rose sharply after the report and remains above $20 but is at high risk of performing poorly as indicated by SA on the CVI Summary page.

Despite the apparent turnaround in stock price performance, I maintain my Sell rating on CVI given the poor macroeconomic outlook for refiners and the even more dire outlook for renewables in 2025 as a result of the new Trump administration policies that will make ongoing investments in renewables even less appealing going forward. In fact, during his remarks on the Q4 earnings call CEO Lamp discussed the headwinds that the company is facing in the renewables business.

Given these headwinds, as the renewable space moves forward, it is difficult to ignore that we have invested approximately $290 million in our renewable business over the last several years to participate in carbon emission reduction to generate RINs and optimize our assets we have. In doing so, we have been reminded that reliance on government credits is not a sustainable business, and we already have enough exposure to politically mismanaged regulations like RFS.

As a result, we are left with an investment with uncertain returns in the business that today is breakeven at best. We have completed the design of SAF RD project near our Coffeyville facilities, have a firm understanding of our capability to convert our Wynnewood renewable diesel unit to SAF production with additional capital.

Potential for these opportunities in the future, it is critical to get clarity on availability and durability of government subsidies before we continue investing additional capital or time into such ventures. We remain willing to participate further in this space but are pausing our intentions to actively pursue the market for partners and investors.

Some additional information from the AI-generated earnings call insights suggested that renewable capacity was decreased in Q4 and that negative catalysts remain putting constraints on renewable feedstock optimization.

  • Renewable diesel production capacity has been reduced to 80 million gallons annually due to catalyst limitations. Management noted a catalyst-related degradation impacted December performance.
  • Matthew Blair, Tudor Pickering: Sought clarity on capture rates and renewable diesel feedstock mix. CFO Neumann noted slight tailwinds to Q4 capture rates due to inventory benefits, and CEO Lamp highlighted catalyst constraints limiting feedstock optimization.

Analyst sentiment was subdued although cautiously optimistic of a market recovery in the refining segment. The SA Quant ratings suggest a Strong Sell with very poor factor grades in nearly every category except Valuation.

Refining Peers are Struggling, Too

CVR Energy is not the only US-based refiner that delivered poor performance over the past year. Several of the company’s peers like Calumet (CLMT), PBF Energy (PBF), and Delek US Holdings (DK) have also reported negative total returns in the last 12 months and are not overly optimistic regarding the 2025 outlook.

In fact, PBF plunged to its lowest level in three years after reporting a larger than expected loss in Q4. Calumet was cut to a Sell rating by UBS due to regulatory uncertainty.

The uncertainty in the renewables segment is especially an issue to watch with CVI and the CEO was somewhat defensive to the point of being almost antagonistic when asked about the outlook for potential opportunities in that segment during the earnings call Q&A:

Q: Adam Wijaya

Fair. And just a follow-up for me on the Renewables segment. I saw that you guys broke the segment earnings profile out this quarter. And when we think about the path to run rate, positive EBITDA contribution going forward, aside from improving margins more broadly. Operationally, is there anything you think we should be focused on?

And then can you tie that into how you guys think about potential opportunities within renewables specifically in SAF kind of what's needed from a supply-demand standpoint or an incentive standpoint to further make a call there?

A: Dave Lamp

Sure, Adam. I mean, as I mentioned in the prepared remarks, is the problem with renewables is the uncertainty of government subsidies. And there they will swing wildly. I guess, I'd say it. And when I say that it's politically driven RFS regulation that seems to be there's nobody driving the ship. And I'll just use the example of the fact that the mandate is 22 billion and 15, 16 of that is ethanol. Ethanol does little for the environment, whereas renewable diesel does.

And lo and behold, you've got D6s coupled with D4s, that's starting to break a little bit. But even so, you would think the government would drive towards lower carbon material rather than just doing a mandate on ethanol, which is really not necessary at all because ethanol is a part of the fuel blend. It's a cheap blend stock for octane, and it would be blended no matter what.

So until we get clarity on some of these regulations and somebody in adults in the room controlling them. It'd be difficult to see how we make some investments. Just to give you an idea on SAF, the subsidies -- today, the people are reporting that that's selling for about $1 to $2 premium over R&D. If you take all the subsidies and add them all up, you need more like $4 to really -- to even have that kind of sales price.

So to us, it's -- we've got -- we've had all we can stand of exposure to government subsidies and it's going to take a shift change for us to really invest in -- we do have projects that look attractive on a capital per barrel capital basis for both SAF and renewables. But again, these subsidies are just scary.

Summary: Avoid CVI Stock

With the new Trump 2.0 administration’s energy policies and plans to roll back some of the IRA legislation passed during the Biden administration, the landscape for renewable energy regulation is changing. This has created consternation and frustration on the part of companies like CVR Energy that have made substantial investments in renewable energy infrastructure over the past several years. Until and unless they can find a buyer for the renewable segment, I believe that CVI stock will continue to underperform and I maintain my Sell rating.

data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Full Year 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Full Year Market Update

U.S. equities posted another strong year of performance in 2024, in almost a carbon copy of 2023's robust gains. An easing of macro risks (lower inflation and a weakening labor market), a shift at the Federal Reserve to less restrictive monetary policy, and a late-year election bump drove market gains. And like last year, there was a wide dichotomy in leadership as the market favored larger cap growth over smaller cap value. While equity markets delivered strong headline gains in 2024, these returns were largely driven by multiple expansion, leaving valuations elevated and the market increasingly reliant on a handful of mega-cap leaders. Looking at market factors, Growth, Volatility, Size, and Momentum factors had the most positive impact on relative returns, while Value, Yield, and most of the Quality factors presented headwinds.

Key Performance Takeaways for the Year

  • The London Company Income Equity portfolio returned 12.3% (11.9% net) year-to-date vs. a 14.4% increase in the Russell 1000 Value Index. Stock selection was a headwind to relative performance, partially offset by positive sector exposure.

  • For the year, the Income Equity portfolio slightly trailed the Russell 1000 Value index, but strong relative performance in the second half brought up our upside participation to 85-90%-in line with expectations. The portfolio made up ground in 3Q as factors shifted our way. However, headwinds from exposure to Quality and Yield factors detracted from relative performance for the year.

  • Our portfolio positioning is always consistent with a focus on risk reduction, and we believe this bodes well for our approach in an expensive, concentrated market. We believe our focus on quality, diversification, and shareholder will continue to reinforce our margin of safety, positioning Income Equity portfolio for success in this uncertain climate.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Progressive Corporation (PGR) - PGR outperformed during the year due to its success in growing the business and improving margins. PGR has gained market share from competitors and improved its margins by effectively segmenting underwriting risks and implementing strategic pricing. PGR has achieved its profitability target by lowering advertising costs and focusing on acquiring preferred customers. We remain confident in PGR's ability to execute in all environments, competitive advantages, and capital allocation strategy.

  • Apple Inc. (AAPL) - Shares of AAPL rallied throughout the year after posting strong results with improvement in China and positive AI enhancements to products. AAPL also announced a $110B share repurchase authorization and a 4% increase in the dividend. iPhone revenue was down a bit although there were some moving pieces that made year-over-year comparisons challenging. Its large installed based and services are an important long-term driver of the business.

  • Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) - Shares of PM outperformed during the year mainly due to strong execution and an improving outlook. The smoke-free category remains the key driver with notable growth from ZYN nicotine pouches. IQOS growth is being fueled by innovation and smoker conversions, while the U.S. presents a major untapped commercialization opportunity. The successful portfolio shift has resulted in smoke-free products now accounting for nearly 40% of total net revenue and gross profit. Guidance points to continued acceleration in reduced-risk product adoption, which should drive overall volume growth and market share gains. We believe the combination of smoke-free growth potential and a resilient combustibles portfolio will generate significant and sustainable free cash flow in the years ahead.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Nestle S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY) - NSRGY shares underperformed the broader market all year. Sentiment across the packaged food space is weak as it emerges from two years of unprecedented food price inflation. NSRGY's latest earnings report did not suggest an acceleration in organic growth from current subdued trends. NSRGY's portfolio is attractively positioned in categories that have stable, long-term volume tailwinds such as coffee, pet food, and nutritional health. Barring further executional missteps, we believe the downside to the current stock price is low.

  • Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - MRK's underperformance for the year reflected a weaker outlook for its HPV vaccine amid challenges in the China market, along with concerns about the incoming administration. Certain pharma stocks have come under pressure since the election and subsequent Presidential nominations for healthcare positions, as the market digests potential policy implications. Despite these headwinds, MRK continues to show strength across its portfolio, particularly with Keytruda, which is driving growth and showing promise in new launches and combination therapies. The company has successfully expanded its pipeline through effective R&D and acquisitions. We believe MRK's strong drug and vaccine portfolio combined with effective leadership positions it well for continued growth.

  • United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) - UPS underperformed this year due to the challenging transportation and parcel market. Many foreign economies have been weak in recent months and economic growth in the U.S. has shifted to services from goods (negative for shipping companies). Weaker revenue growth along with higher union contract wages led to lower margins. While recent results have been lackluster, we maintain our confidence in the long-term growth opportunities in the global package delivery business. The dividend yield is roughly 5% and management continues to return cash to shareholders via dividends and share repurchase at attractive prices.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Health Care & Energy (two weaker performing sectors)

  • What Hurt: Underweight Utilities (a better performing sector) & overweight Information Technology (a weaker performing sector)

Portfolio Characteristics & Positioning

We believe the Income Equity portfolio is positioned for long-term durability and possesses the fundamental ingredients that stand the test of time: wide moats, sustainably high returns on capital, strong free cash flow generation, low leverage ratios, and an attractive shareholder yield (dividends + net buybacks). The portfolio trades at a premium to the Value index, but we believe this is justified as companies in the Value indices have lower returns on capital and higher leverage. As a corporate debt maturity wall approaches and the cost of that capital stays elevated, we believe companies with strong balance sheets and the ability to self-fund their operations could have a structural advantage in 2025 and beyond. In an environment of possibly lower returns and greater volatility, we believe the Income Equity portfolio offers an attractive option for equity investors.

Looking Ahead

As we enter 2025, we believe the market faces an inflection point where sustaining momentum becomes increasingly difficult. Across the real economy, demand still seems sluggish and clear late-cycle signals persist. Revenue growth and corporate profits have leaned on inflationary pricing, but margins face growing headwinds as inflationary pricing fades, input costs rise, and demand softens. The Fed cut rates during 2024, but the yields on longer-dated treasuries actually rose as the year ended. Stubbornly high borrowing costs continue to plague rate-sensitive areas of the economy, like housing. Employment and inflation data may be volatile in 2025 and could affect changes in monetary policy and lead to greater volatility across equity markets.

Despite resilient economic data and limited signs of credit risk, we believe vigilance is warranted. Our cautious posture persists due to high valuations, market concentration, looming debt challenges, and fraying consumer health. We anticipate lower expected returns in the near term, based on slowing growth (function of restrictive monetary policy) and high valuations. Valuation multiple expansion can only take the market so far (particularly late in a market cycle). We expect a reversion to the mean whereby earnings growth & dividends drive returns going forward. While optimism remains high, the vulnerabilities of momentum-driven leadership highlight the need for discipline. Markets may reward risk-taking in the short term, but lasting wealth is built through patience, real income, and fundamentals.

Annualized Returns

As of 12/31/2024

Inception date: 12/31/1999. Past performance should not be taken as a guarantee of future results. Performance is preliminary. Subject to change.

Disclosure Notes

The London Company's performances are size weighted and annualized based on calculations for the period ending December 31, 2024. The characteristics discussed herein relate to a representative account, and not every client's account will have these exact characteristics. As London manages its client portfolios according to each client's specific investment needs and circumstances, London cannot affirm that the characteristics of the account shown are similar to all accounts participating in the strategy. This is due in part to the timing of trades by the Advisor, market conditions, cash availability, and the timing of client deposits and withdrawals. Therefore, prospective clients should not assume that similar performance results to those shown would have been achieved for their accounts had they been invested in the strategy during the period. None of the information contained herein should be construed as an offer to buy or sell securities, or as investment recommendations.

Definition of Firm: The London Company of Virginia is a registered investment advisor. Registration does not imply a certain level of skill or training. More information about the advisor, including full descriptions of its investment strategies, fees and objectives, can be found in the firm's Form ADV Part 2, which is available upon request by calling 804.775.0317 or visiting www.TLCadvisory.com. The London Company claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®). GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein. Please visit www.TLCadvisory.com or contact us at 804.775.0317 to request a complete list and description of The London Company's composites and/or a presentation that adheres to the (GIPS®) standards.

Composite Creation/Inception Date: December 31, 1999

Composite Definition: The Income Equity strategy invests mainly in common equities with a focus on higher overall dividend yield orientation, which may be supplemented with primarily investment grade, preferred equities. This strategy has a more conservative orientation, with a focus on capital preservation, income and growth, in order to provide greater yield and downside protection relative to our Large and Mid Cap strategies. Our Income Equity strategy is designed to generate above-average, absolute returns over full market cycles. Accounts in this product composite are fully discretionary taxable and tax-exempt portfolios with no minimum dollar amount of assets. The product is measured against the Russell 1000 Value Index and has a creation and inception date of December 31, 1999. There is no use of leverage, derivatives or short positions. All actual fee-paying discretionary portfolios are included in one or more composites that have been managed for a full calendar quarter with limited restrictions and similar objectives. As of July 1, 2022 The London Company redefined the composites to exclude all dual contract relationships and any potentially bundled fee scenarios. This policy is not retroactive, but will continue to apply going forward.

Benchmark Description: The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. Benchmark returns are not covered by the report of independent verifiers.

Performance and Fees: Gross of fee returns are calculated gross of management and custodian fees and net of transaction costs. Net of fee returns are calculated net of actual management fees and transaction costs and gross of custodian and other fees. Returns may be net of miscellaneous fund expenses. The gross figures do not reflect the deduction of investment advisory fees. Returns are calculated and stated in U.S. dollars. Returns are calculated gross of withholding taxes on foreign dividends and interest. Dividends are reinvested. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request.

Past performance should not be taken as a guarantee of future results. The report is for informational purposes only. Data, while obtained from sources we believe to be reliable, cannot be guaranteed and all statistics are subject to change. The statements contained herein are solely based upon the opinions of The London Company and the data available at the time of publication of this report, and there is no assurance that any predicted results will actually occur. Information was obtained from third-party sources, which we believe to be reliable but are not guaranteed as to their accuracy or completeness. This report contains no recommendations to buy or sell any specific securities and should not be considered investment advice of any kind. An investment in a London Company strategy is subject to risks, including the loss of principal. Referenced strategies may not be suitable for all investors. The appropriateness of a particular strategy will depend on individual circumstances and objectives. In making an investment decision, individuals should utilize other information sources and the advice of their investment advisor.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
